name: Ghent

overview.description: Ghent is a lively university city built around its medieval core, known for its canals, guild houses and the 'three towers' skyline of St. Bavo's Cathedral, the Belfry and St. Nicholas' Church. A large student population gives it a younger, less touristy feel than nearby Bruges.
overview.foundedNote: A major trading city since the Middle Ages, historically one of Europe's largest cities north of the Alps
whyVisit: Gravensteen — the only fully preserved moated medieval castle in Flanders
Graslei and Korenlei — twin quays lined with 12th-century guild houses along the Leie river
St. Bavo's Cathedral, home to the Ghent Altarpiece by Jan van Eyck
The Belfry of Ghent — at ~91m, the tallest belfry in Belgium and a UNESCO World Heritage Site
A large, lively student population that keeps the city more affordable and less touristy than Bruges

attractions.name: Gravensteen (Castle of the Counts)
attractions.description: Built around 1180 for the Counts of Flanders, this is the only fully preserved moated castle in Flanders. An audio guide covers its medieval history.

attractions.name: Graslei & Korenlei
attractions.description: Historic twin quays along the Leie river lined with 12th-century guild houses — the postcard view of Ghent, especially at night when the buildings are lit.

attractions.name: St. Bavo's Cathedral
attractions.description: Gothic cathedral housing the Ghent Altarpiece ('The Adoration of the Mystic Lamb') by Jan van Eyck, one of the most important works in Western art history.

attractions.name: Belfry of Ghent
attractions.description: At roughly 91 metres, the tallest belfry in Belgium, built in the 14th century as a symbol of civic independence; part of the UNESCO-listed Belfries of Belgium and France.

transportation.description: Ghent has a well-connected tram network run by De Lijn covering the historic centre and university areas.
tips: Buy tickets via the De Lijn app before boarding for the best price
The historic centre is largely pedestrianised — walking is often fastest

transportation.description: Ghent-Sint-Pieters connects directly to Brussels (~30-35 min), Antwerp and Bruges.
Ghent is very bike-friendly with extensive cycle lanes and a bike-share scheme.

hotels.name: Historic Centre (Patershol / Korenmarkt)
hotels.notes: Walkable to Gravensteen, Graslei and the main squares; generally cheaper than equivalent Bruges hotels.

foodSpots.name: Indian and international restaurants near the university district
foodSpots.notes: Ghent's large student population supports a diverse, affordable food scene around Overpoort and the city centre; dedicated Indian restaurant options are more limited than in Brussels or Antwerp.

tips: Ghent is very bike-friendly — consider renting a bike to see the city like a local.
The Gent India Dans Festival, curated by the Ghent-based Indian dance company ABC (A Bollywood Company), brings Bollywood, Bhangra and Garba performances to the city.
Less touristy and generally cheaper than Bruges, making it a good base for a longer stay in Flanders.
